originalTitle: Trainspotting the Musical review – forgettable songs make this a bad trip
summary: Theatre Royal Haymarket, London The chopped-up story means this ends up feeling like a weak impersonation of Danny Boyle’s classic film
content: “Choose life …” Those iconic words from that era-defining film are said more than once in this musical take on Irvine Welsh’s novel about Renton and his renegade gang of smackheads from Leith. “Choose being in the moment,” says the disembodied voice that asks us to switch off our mobile phones before the show has begun (with, one imagines, a self-satisfied glint at the joke). “Choose life,” repeats Renton (Lewis Kidd), trying so hard, it seems, to capture the dangerous energy of that moment in the 1990s when Ewan McGregor, Jonny Lee Miller and co depicted heroin addiction as a protest against the grunting conventions of ordinary life. Sadly, the drug-addled escapades and illegal highs here look about as dangerous as a double-dose of Gaviscon. Written by Welsh and directed by Caroline Jay Ranger, the story appears chopped up so that one scene is disconnected to the next, the story bitty, the well-known scenes and lines rattled off as if from a Trainspotting-branded assembly line, albeit in a different order: first the group trip to Blackpool for a northern soul gig, then the various set-pieces, from the speed-fuelled job interview by Spud (Kieran Andrew) to the snooker-table violence from Begbie (Frankie O’Connor) and Renton’s toilet high – the latter made more vivid with the use of back screens (video design by Douglas O’Connell). There are some touching moments with the death of Alison’s (Rosie Dignan) baby and the decline of the most well-adjusted of the group, Tommy (Finlay Paul), until he discovers heroin, but these are not nearly as devastating as they should be, and are briskly enacted. None of it is half as shocking, entertaining or as poignant as the film. Nor is the imagination as sparkling when it goes its own way, such as a moment in which Spud’s mother rails against social ills (from prescription drugs to sugar and additives). The advertising declares the return of these characters, 30 years on from Danny Boyle’s film, but that’s not quite true because this train has been chugging on ever since the publication of Welsh’s novel in 1993, which has spawned a prequel, three sequels, a stage play, and a second film (T2 Trainspotting). It could be Trainspotting Inc, never ending in its mutant life forms. The leads are mutants of sorts here, visibly styled on the film actors, albeit cardboard flat: Renton with shaved head and jeans but without the dark charisma of McGregor. It is hardly Kidd’s fault – characterisation doesn’t allow nuance or individuality. Sick Boy, cocksure, with dyed blond hair; Spud a twitching dork and Begbie a pub psychopath with a moustache. In its music and songs, the cover numbers bring the few high points. There is Iggy Pop’s Lust for Life, so synonymous with the film that you can still picture McGregor’s sprint along the streets of Edinburgh, and renditions of Lou Reed’s Perfect Day. But, much like the musical itself, they do nothing more than imitate a far better original, already out there. Alongside the covers are songs written by Stephen McGuinness and Welsh which are forgettable at best, or best forgotten in the case of one with a country and western twang and another which sounds alarmingly like a riff on The Proclaimers. It’s strange to see these characters singing as they shoot up, or rage against the machine. “Would Renton really choose to return to this life,” you find yourself wondering. It all seems so tame and commercial for this bunch of anti-capitalists. A bad trip indeed. Plays until 5 September followed by UK tour
chineseTitle: 猜火车音乐评论——容易忘记的歌曲让这次旅行很糟糕
chineseSummary: 伦敦皇家干草市场剧院 支离破碎的故事意味着这最终感觉像是对丹尼·博伊尔经典电影的微弱模仿
chineseContent: “选择生活……”这部划时代电影中的标志性词语在这部音乐剧中多次出现，改编自欧文·威尔士的小说，讲述了伦顿和他的利斯叛徒团伙的故事。 “选择活在当下，”那个虚无缥缈的声音要求我们在演出开始前关掉手机（人们可以想象，这个笑话中带着一丝自鸣得意的光芒）。 “选择生活”，伦顿（刘易斯·基德饰）重复道，他似乎非常努力地捕捉 20 世纪 90 年代那一刻的危险能量，当时伊万·麦格雷戈、约翰·李·米勒等人将海洛因成瘾描述为对日常生活中令人厌烦的习俗的抗议。可悲的是，这里的吸毒越轨和非法兴奋看起来就像服用双倍剂量的 Gaviscon 一样危险。这部由威尔士编剧、卡罗琳·杰·兰杰执导的故事看起来支离破碎，一个场景与另一个场景互不相干，故事情节琐碎，众所周知的场景和台词就像是来自猜火车品牌的装配线一样，尽管顺序不同：首先是集体前往布莱克浦参加北方灵魂演出，然后是各种定位球，从斯普德（基兰·安德鲁饰）的快速面试到斯诺克台球桌贝格比（弗兰基·奥康纳饰）和伦顿厕所的暴力行为——后者通过使用后屏幕变得更加生动（道格拉斯·奥康奈尔的视频设计）。艾莉森（罗西·迪格南饰）的孩子去世，汤米（芬利·保罗饰）在这群人中适应能力最强的人汤米（芬利·保罗饰）在发现海洛因之前逐渐衰落，这其中有一些感人的时刻，但这些时刻并没有应有的那么具有破坏性，而且表现得很快。这些都没有电影那么令人震惊、有趣或辛酸。当想象力按照自己的方式行事时，也不会那么闪闪发光，比如斯普德的母亲抱怨的那一刻
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
